g.data-narration: Welcome to Exception Handling in Java. This comprehensive lesson covers everything from basic concepts to advanced exception patterns.
An exception is a runtime error that disrupts program flow. Java's exception handling framework manages these errors systematically using object-oriented principles.
g.text: An exception is a runtime event that disrupts normal program flow.
Java treats errors as objects, enabling systematic error management.
Without handling, exceptions cause program termination.

g.data-narration: Exception handling provides three key benefits: fault isolation separates error code from business logic, error recovery allows programs to continue, and diagnostics provide debugging information.

The Java exception hierarchy starts with Throwable, branching into Error (unrecoverable) and Exception (recoverable). Exception splits into RuntimeException (unchecked) and other exceptions (checked).
Checked exceptions must be declared or handled at compile time (like IOException). Unchecked exceptions (RuntimeException and Error) don't require declaration and occur during runtime.
g.text: Must be declared or handled • Subclasses of Exception

g.data-narration: The try-catch-finally block is the core mechanism. Try contains risky code, catch handles specific exceptions, and finally executes cleanup code always, regardless of exceptions.
Java 7 introduced multi-catch, allowing you to catch multiple exception types in a single catch block using the pipe (|) operator, with implicitly final catch parameter.
g.text: Single catch parameter handles multiple types

g.data-narration: The throws keyword declares exceptions a method may throw (propagation), while throw explicitly creates and throws a new exception instance.
Custom exceptions extend Exception (checked) or RuntimeException (unchecked). They provide application-specific error handling with meaningful messages and optional cause chaining.
Uncaught exceptions propagate up the call stack. Each method can catch and handle or let it propagate. The first matching catch block in the stack handles the exception.
Common exceptions include NullPointerException (unchecked), ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException (unchecked), NumberFormatException (unchecked), and FileNotFoundException (checked).
Exception handling is crucial in real-world Java: database operations use try-with-resources for transactions, file handling manages I/O safely, and network operations handle connectivity errors.
Key syntax includes: try-catch-finally blocks, throws in method declarations, throw for explicit exceptions, and try-with-resources for automatic resource management.

Remember: catch specific exceptions before generic ones, never swallow exceptions with empty catch blocks, use finally or try-with-resources for cleanup, and handle exceptions at the appropriate level.
g.text: Catch exceptions from specific to general
Avoid empty catch blocks - at least log errors
Handle at appropriate call stack level
